name: Aelaerem
description:
Aelaerem

Key Demographic: Aelaerem (Halfling)

Captial: Amedell

Entanglements

To the North East:
Valewood – The downs rise toward the forest; the Green Gate sometimes opens onto halfling barrows. The Hollow's attention is a shared dread.
To the North:
Aelinnel – The gnomes trade in copper and courtesy; the two small-folk peoples share the red thread custom but distrust the fae. The barrows and the fae courts have an unspoken treaty.
To the West:
The Dolmis Sea – Coastal fishing and rare trade with Zakov; the Hollow's attention does not cross salt water easily, but the tide brings strange things ashore.
To the South West:
Theona (within the Dolmis) – The Green Host's riders do not cross the water, but the Ninth Taboo echoes in the halfling tongue. The ninth cup is never poured on either shore.

Quick Reference

Tagline: The hollow counts your steps. The thread remembers your name.

Apple-Matron (Elite):

“A proper harvest feast requires three things: the finest cider aged in the right barrel, a guest-loaf baked with intention, and the wisdom to know when to invite the quiet ones to sit by the fire.”

Miller (Commoner):

“My geese know a stranger's step from a mile off, and they've never been wrong. If they start honking at noon, you best check your larder – someone's coming who wasn't invited. And when the Hollow walks, the only safe place is a lit hearth with the ninth cup unpoured.”

Genre / Mood: Pastoral folk horror, hearth magic, the weight of thresholds.

Starting Location: A cider-press barn perfumed with crushed apples and rain, where masks hang from beams awaiting the next festival. The Apple-Matron sits on a stool of woven twigs. Behind her, a Moot Oak leaf bleeding red sap onto a stone. “The Hollow walks early this year,” she says. “The Moot Oak bleeds wine. Will you speak for the field, or let the Neighbors claim it? Speak true—the geese are listening.”

Theme & Atmosphere

In Amedell, the Aelaerem keep their bonds with bread, bells, and careful counting. Lanes run under ash and hawthorn; doors reddened with thread promise a seat and a story to any who step right. Their magic is housekeeping writ large — thresholds groomed, names jarred like jam, the Hollow fed with silence and the ninth cup left unpoured. When the Neighbors stir beneath the barrows, the folk answer with kettle songs and lantern law, and the fields behave because someone asked them to. Hospitality here is not kindness—it is a covenant. Every meal is a promise, every gift a thread, every candle an argument against the dark.

Amedell lies south of the Aelinnel homeland of Aevrossa, separated by the rolling downs and the slow waters of the River Adder. The two small-folk peoples share trade in red thread, copper, and seasonal hospitality, though the Aelaerem keep a wary distance from the fae-touched gnomish courts. It lies on the southeastern coast of the Dolmis, although the Aelaerem are rarely out to sea beyond coastal fishing.

What you notice first: The smell of baking bread and old apples. The way every threshold has a red thread tied at eye level. The silence of children when a stranger passes the village stone.

The one rule that kills newcomers: Never refuse an offered cup. The Hollow counts refusals as invitations. Pour the ninth cup for the empty chair, and you may feed it—or feed it.

Regional Mood: Warm Hearths, Cold Thresholds

Here, the land is not wilderness—it is a living weave of favors and omissions. The Aelaerem do not build walls; they build hedges, stiles, and the careful geometry of guest-right. A red door means bread, salt, and one safe night if you come honest. A cup-mark stone on the verge accepts butter as a prayer. The watch-geese know your step before you do, and the scarecrow turns to face the road when the Hollow is hungry. The Neighbors—the things that live under the barrows, in the uncounted ninth step, in the space between the candle and its shadow—are not enemies. They are neighbors. And neighbors, if treated politely, will stay on their side of the hedge.

The Hollow's Attention (Campaign Timer )

The Hollow is not a monster. It is a forgetfulness that lives in thresholds, miscounts, and broken guest-right. Track its attention with a visible timer:

Hollow Attention [8] – Advances when:

  • A character refuses an offered cup (+1)
  • A promise is broken on a threshold (+1)
  • The ninth cup is poured (+2)
  • A guest-right is violated (GM spends 1 SB to tick +1)
  • A child points at an empty chair (the Hollow is already listening; +0, but the GM gains 1 SB)

At 4 segments: Lanterns flicker without wind. Dogs whine at nothing. The Hollow is watching.

At 8 segments: The Hollow manifests. Roll on the Hollow Walks table. The party must offer a story, a memory, or a name to appease it.

Hollow Walks (d6)

When the Hollow Attention timer reaches 8, roll 1d6 for the manifestation:

  1. A pale figure stands at the edge of the circle. It asks for a name. Give a name (living or dead) and it leaves. Refuse, and it takes your shadow (–1 die on stealth for a week).
  2. The ninth cup pours itself. Whoever drinks from it forgets their greatest joy (lose 1 XP, refundable by a quest).
  3. A red thread appears tied around every PC's wrist. It tightens when you lie. Cutting it costs 1 Corruption.
  4. The geese fall silent. The Hollow is inside the circle. It demands a story that ends with a death. If the story is true, it leaves. If false, it takes a memory.
  5. The Moot Oak bleeds sap. The Hollow speaks through it: “One of you carries a promise unpaid. Settle it now.” The one named must offer 1 Boon or 1 Supply. Refusal ticks the timer again immediately.
  6. The empty chair is occupied by a figure in gray. It does not speak. It eats. If you serve it food, it leaves. If you ignore it, it eats your luck (all rolls are Desperate for the next scene).

Guest-Right Violations (d6)

When a PC breaks guest-right (refuses a cup, draws steel under a red door, insults the host), roll 1d6:

center

d6Consequence
1The host demands a price of 1 Supply (the cost of a broken loaf).
2The village stones turn inward. You are not welcome anywhere for a week (–1 Position on all social rolls).
3A red thread ties itself around your wrist. It tightens when you speak to an Aelaerem. Cut it with a silver knife (cost 1 Boon) or suffer –1 die on all interactions.
4The watch-geese chase you from the village. Gain a Marked by Geese condition (animals are hostile; +1 DV to all Beastcraft rolls).
5The Hollow notices you. Tick Hollow Attention +2. The empty chair will be waiting at your next camp.
6The Apple-Matron herself banishes you. Start a Gate of Thorns [4] timer — when full, you cannot enter any Aelaerem village until you pay a blood-price.

center

Prominent NPCs of Aelaerem

Name

Role/TitleMotivationRumored Quirk
Oria “Crimson Door”Apple-Matron of the Harvest MootKeep the Hollow at bay and the feasts trueShe pours the first cup for the eldest, the ninth for the empty chair. Never takes the ninth herself.
BramMiller of the Watch-GeeseProtect the village from unseen strangersHis geese have names; one is “Stranger,” and it has never been wrong.
CeliaHedge-Witch MidwifeTie red thread and untie feuds before they festerHer thread is from her own hair. She never cuts it—only knots it.
DrogoThresher-King (seasonal)Lead the harvest rites and keep the sheaf's secretChosen by the last sheaf, which is always a little damp.
AldaLantern-Warden of the DownsTrim the lamps and know which shadows are wrongLights them in a pattern that is a prayer.
FinnianMummers' CaptainGuard the masks and the laws of festivalThe masks are not carved. They are grown.

Names of Aelaerem

Inspired by the pastoral hill country of a green and misty isle: earthy, clipped, with soft vowels and hard consonants, evoking hearth, hedge, and hollow.

(Lanes/Orchards/Mills/Downs/Barrows)

Willow ford with flat stones and a bowed pollard that remembers faces; cider-press barn with sweet reek, old songs, doors that stick after dusk; chalk sheep-downs with a turf maze cut like a wheel.
